title: Why Some Mountains Keep Growing Today

lesson.learn:
Some mountain ranges are still growing because the tectonic forces that built them have not finished. If plates keep converging or the crust is still rebounding and thickening, uplift can continue even while erosion tries to wear the range down. Mountains are always in a tug-of-war between building and destruction.

That means the range we see today is just a snapshot. The rocks are still moving, even if the motion is too slow for human eyes.
lesson.simpler: Some mountains keep growing because tectonic forces are still pushing the crust upward.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Mountain growth continues wherever uplift outpaces erosion.

thirdImageCredit.creator: Lyn Topinka, United States Geological Survey. Modified from Plank and Schenck, 1998, Delaware Piedmont Geology, Delaware Geological Society.

lesson.deeperDive:
Ranges can keep rising for several reasons: crustal shortening at a collision zone, subduction-related compression, mantle dynamics, or isostatic rebound after ice or erosion removes weight from the crust. The exact mechanism depends on the region. In places like the Himalayas, plate collision is still active. In others, old mountain belts may rise slowly because the crust is still adjusting.

So mountains are not finished products. They are processes made visible.
lesson.funFact: Some mountain ranges are still being pushed upward right now.

people: Glaciologists - scientists tracking erosion by ice
Mountain communities - people living with changing landscapes
places: The Himalayas - one of the fastest-growing ranges
Fold belts - classic compressional mountain zones
Active fault systems - places where mountains can rise
years: 2000s - mountain growth is measured more precisely
Present day - ranges are tracked in real time
Future - mountain building will continue as long as plates move
concepts: Erosion - the force that counteracts growth
